pick.rationale: St. John's holds a significant advantage in overall efficiency metrics, ranking 17-20th in KenPom and Torvik compared to Northern Iowa's 70-71st range, reflecting the substantial gap between Big East and Missouri Valley Conference competition levels. The Red Storm enter as Big East Tournament champions with elite defensive efficiency (12th nationally) and key players like Kadary Richmond and Zuby Ejiofor healthy and available. While Northern Iowa presents legitimate upset danger as a hot 12-seed winner of five straight games including the MVC Tournament, their offensive limitations (151st-153rd ranked) against St. John's superior defense should prove decisive in this competitive matchup.

reasoningStep.title: St. John's vs Northern Iowa Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: St. John's favored based on efficiency gap, defensive excellence, and Big East pedigree despite Northern Iowa's momentum
reasoningStep.evidence: St. John's ranks 17-20th in major efficiency metrics (KenPom 20, Torvik 18) with elite defense (12th adjDE) and 27-6 to 28-6 record as Big East champions. Northern Iowa ranks 70-71st with strong defense (26th adjDE) but weaker offense (151st-153th adjOE), entering on five-game winning streak after MVC Tournament title. Bracketologists lists St. John's as 10.5-point favorite. Key St. John's players Richmond and Ejiofor available; Northern Iowa led by Trey Campbell who scored 23 points in MVC championship.

result.text: Ben Jacobson, UNI basketball gear up for St. John's in March Madness Skip to main content Home Sports Hawk Central Big Ten Sports Cyclone Insider High School Insider SportsbookWire Odds USA TODAY Sports Recruiting All Iowa Sports Awards Can UNI men convert end-of-season success into March Madness win? Alyssa Hertel Des Moines Register Show Caption Hide Caption Hear from Ben Jacobson after UNI basketball's win over Drake The University of Northern Iowa basketball coach Ben Jacobson breaks down the Panthers win over Drake on Sunday. For the first time in 10 years, the Northern Iowa men’s basketball team is dancing. The Panthers – who spent the season bouncing back from a few impactful injuries – took home the Missouri Valley Conference Tournament championship, earning an automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament. No. 12-seeded UNI is set to face fifth-seed St. John’s on Friday, March 20 at 6:10 p.m. CT in San Diego, Calif. But leading up to that announcement, there was a week full of peaks and valleys. In Division I men’s basketball, the Missouri Valley hosts one of the earliest conference tournaments – with a week between the champion being crowned and Selection Sunday kicking off. So, after becoming the first team in conference history to win four games in four days to claim the trophy, the waiting game began for Northern Iowa. No set seeding; no known opponent; no regional destination. Just a moment in between the two most important points of the season to get back to the fun of the game. The Panthers, who were the first 6-seed to win the Valley Tournament, took off the two days following the conference championship game. They returned to the gym on Wednesday, March 11, for some light workouts and a session in the weight room. UNI resumed practice on Thursday, Friday and Saturday before enjoying another day off on Sunday. "It’s such a great week because you know you’re in the NCAA Tournament,” UNI coach Ben Jacobson said Monday, March 16. “It’s a really neat opportunity for our players and for our program to have, when you’re not playing a game.” And then Selection Sunday rolled around, and one thing after the other – all out of the team's control – seemed to go wrong. The Panthers’ public watch party at a local events center flipped to a private event – players, coaches, support staff and their families – at McLeod Center, due to safety concerns surrounding a blizzard warning impacting the state. UNI was among the first teams revealed on the bracket but – due to an error with the live feed from CBS Sports – the Panthers learned their seeding, opponent and destination after fans watching on TV did. Did everything go according to plan? No, but that didn’t take away from the Panthers enjoying the moment – Northern Iowa’s first appearance in March Madness since 2016. “I talked to the guys … I said, even though (we know) our name’s coming up, it’s (still) an unbelievable feeling,” Jacobson said. “Then they go, and they have the technical difficulty, and

result.text: Ben Jacobson, UNI basketball gear up for St. John's in March Madness Skip to main content Home Sports Hawk Central Big Ten Sports Cyclone Insider High School Insider SportsbookWire Odds USA TODAY Sports Recruiting All Iowa Sports Awards Can UNI men convert end-of-season success into March Madness win? Alyssa Hertel Des Moines Register Show Caption Hide Caption Hear from Ben Jacobson after UNI basketball's win over Drake The University of Northern Iowa basketball coach Ben Jacobson breaks down the Panthers win over Drake on Sunday. For the first time in 10 years, the Northern Iowa men’s basketball team is dancing. The Panthers – who spent the season bouncing back from a few impactful injuries – took home the Missouri Valley Conference Tournament championship, earning an automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament. No. 12-seeded UNI is set to face fifth-seed St. John’s on Friday, March 20 at 6:10 p.m. CT in San Diego, Calif. But leading up to that announcement, there was a week full of peaks and valleys. In Division I men’s basketball, the Missouri Valley hosts one of the earliest conference tournaments – with a week between the champion being crowned and Selection Sunday kicking off. So, after becoming the first team in conference history to win four games in four days to claim the trophy, the waiting game began for Northern Iowa. No set seeding; no known opponent; no regional destination. Just a moment in between the two most important points of the season to get back to the fun of the game. The Panthers, who were the first 6-seed to win the Valley Tournament, took off the two days following the conference championship game. They returned to the gym on Wednesday, March 11, for some light workouts and a session in the weight room. UNI resumed practice on Thursday, Friday and Saturday before enjoying another day off on Sunday. "It’s such a great week because you know you’re in the NCAA Tournament,” UNI coach Ben Jacobson said Monday, March 16. “It’s a really neat opportunity for our players and for our program to have, when you’re not playing a game.” And then Selection Sunday rolled around, and one thing after the other – all out of the team's control – seemed to go wrong. The Panthers’ public watch party at a local events center flipped to a private event – players, coaches, support staff and their families – at McLeod Center, due to safety concerns surrounding a blizzard warning impacting the state. UNI was among the first teams revealed on the bracket but – due to an error with the live feed from CBS Sports – the Panthers learned their seeding, opponent and destination after fans watching on TV did. Did everything go according to plan? No, but that didn’t take away from the Panthers enjoying the moment – Northern Iowa’s first appearance in March Madness since 2016. “I talked to the guys … I said, even though (we know) our name’s coming up, it’s (still) an unbelievable feeling,” Jacobson said. “Then they go, and they have the technical difficulty, and
